reasoning: The Bank Prime Loan Rate moves in lockstep with the Federal Funds Target Range, specifically sitting 3.00% above the target range's upper limit. As of June 22, 2025, the rate is 7.50%, corresponding to a 4.25%-4.50% Fed Funds range. For the prime rate to increase, the FOMC must pass a rate hike. The next scheduled FOMC meetings after the June 18 decision are late July (Jul 29-30), September (Sep 16-17), October (Oct 28-29), and December (Dec 9-10). The June 29 and July 22 resolution dates fall before the next scheduled meeting (July 29-30), making an increase dependent on an extremely unlikely inter-meeting (emergency) rate hike; hence the probability is set to the near-minimum of 5%. For September 20 and December 19, CME FedWatch data as of mid-June 2025 indicates that the market overwhelming expects 'cuts' (approx. 88% chance of at least two cuts by year-end). Any chance of an interest rate hike relies on an unexpected severe inflation rebound. Because forecasting inflation long-term is uncertain, the estimated probability of a rate increase rises modestly to 8% by September and 14% by the end of December, acknowledging the widening uncertainty fan while remaining well below 50%.
